critic_raw:
1. APPLYING A STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT TO AN UNSUPERVISED ANOMALY DETECTION MODEL IS CONCEPTUALLY INCOHERENT. The design uses an autoencoder as the anomaly detection model. Autoencoders for anomaly detection are trained on normal data only — they learn to reconstruct normal operating patterns and identify anomalies by high reconstruction error. A stratified random split that ensures fault events are 'proportionately represented across training, validation, and test sets' would place fault instances in the training set, which contaminates the normal-data training assumption for the autoencoder. If the autoencoder is trained on data that includes fault events, it learns to reconstruct fault signatures as well as normal ones, degrading anomaly detection sensitivity. Evidence: verify whether fault-labeled data points are included in or excluded from autoencoder training; if included, the training procedure contradicts the unsupervised anomaly detection premise.

2. MEAN TIME TO FAILURE (MTTF) AS AN EVALUATION METRIC REQUIRES CAUSAL INFERENCE ABOUT INTERVENTION TIMING THAT THE DESIGN CANNOT SUPPORT. MTTF measures the time between failure events. Comparing MTTF under the autoencoder versus the threshold baseline requires knowing what would have happened without intervention — i.e., the counterfactual equipment lifetime under no alert. If both systems generate alerts that trigger maintenance, MTTF improvement may reflect maintenance scheduling practices rather than model quality. The design does not describe how maintenance interventions are triggered by model alerts or how MTTF is computed in a way that isolates model contribution from maintenance response. Evidence: specify the intervention policy (what actions are triggered by an alert) and how MTTF is estimated from the historical data without prospective experiments.

3. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON TIME-SERIES SENSOR DATA CREATES PRE-FAILURE TRAJECTORY LEAKAGE. Equipment sensor readings leading up to a fault exhibit a recognizable degradation trajectory. A random split will assign readings from the same pre-failure trajectory to both training and test sets. For the threshold-based baseline, this is less problematic since thresholds do not memorize trajectories. For the autoencoder, pre-failure readings that appear in the training set will affect the learned reconstruction distribution, making pre-failure readings from the same degradation episode in the test set easier to detect as anomalies — not because the model generalizes, but because it memorized the trajectory. Evidence: use a chronological split (train on equipment history before cutoff, test on equipment history after) and compare detection performance.

4. 'PRECISION AT TOP-K ANOMALIES' AS A SECONDARY METRIC REQUIRES KNOWING THE ANOMALY THRESHOLD FOR K, WHICH IS CIRCULAR FOR AN UNSUPERVISED MODEL. The autoencoder detects anomalies by reconstruction error exceeding a threshold. Precision at top-K anomalies requires ranking all test points by reconstruction error and evaluating whether the top K are true faults. This ranking is valid, but the 'K' must be chosen independently of the test data. If K is set based on the expected number of faults in the test set (derived from the stratified split proportions), then K is calibrated using the test labels, which constitutes using test information to set the evaluation criterion. Evidence: specify how K is determined for the precision@K calculation and confirm it is set before examining test labels.

5. STRATIFYING BY TIME-OF-DAY AND OPERATING CONDITIONS DOES NOT ADDRESS TEMPORAL NON-STATIONARITY IN EQUIPMENT DEGRADATION. The design claims to mitigate confounds by 'stratifying data based on time of day and operating conditions.' Equipment in industrial settings undergoes wear over time — an autoencoder trained on equipment in early-life operation will behave differently on equipment in end-of-life operation even under identical operating conditions. Temporal non-stationarity in the underlying equipment state is not addressed by conditioning on operating conditions. A model trained on the first half of the 12-month dataset may not perform well on the second half if equipment wear has progressed. Evidence: compare autoencoder performance on early versus late portions of the test period; if performance degrades over time, temporal equipment aging is a confound.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split that places fault instances in autoencoder training data contradicts unsupervised anomaly detection premise
MTTF as an evaluation metric requires counterfactual reasoning about intervention timing that the design cannot support
Random split creates pre-failure trajectory leakage in autoencoder training
Precision at top-K requires K to be determined before examining test labels — circular if K is derived from stratified fault counts
Temporal equipment aging creates non-stationarity not addressed by operating-condition stratification
Stratified random split ensures fault examples in training set — contradicts unsupervised anomaly detection requirement to train on normal data only
MTTF as primary metric cannot be computed from offline classification evaluation without production deployment or counterfactual simulation
Threshold tuning comparability unspecified — autoencoder threshold may be label-informed while baseline threshold is expert-defined, creating evaluation asymmetry
Internal inconsistency between binary threshold optimization and precision-at-top-K secondary metric requiring different operating points
Twelve-month within-year split cannot assess generalization to seasonal or operational regime shifts not represented in training
Stratified split includes faults in autoencoder training, violating the unsupervised anomaly detection assumption that training data is normal
MTTF is a system reliability metric, not a model evaluation metric; category error in primary metric selection
Random split fragments fault trajectories, causing temporal leakage specific to degradation-pattern detection
Equivalent tuning effort between autoencoder and threshold-based system is undefined given incommensurable parameter spaces
Hyperparameter tuning on labeled validation data makes the unsupervised autoencoder implicitly supervised, contradicting the method framing
